result.markdown:
--- title: "P&C Severity CPI: A Claims-Weighted Inflation Index" date: "2025-02-01" categories: [actuarial, inflation, visualization, insurance] --- ## The Problem with CPI-U for Insurance The standard Consumer Price Index (CPI-U) is designed to measure inflation for typical household consumption. But for Property & Casualty insurance actuaries, it significantly underweights the cost components that actually drive claims severity: - **Medical care** gets only ~6% weight in CPI-U, while bodily injury claims can be 30-50% of auto liability losses - **Shelter** receives ~33% but doesn't reflect specific materials and labor costs in property claims - **Auto repair**, hospital costs, and physician fees are underrepresented relative to claims exposure This creates a systematic gap when using CPI-U for loss trending and rate adequacy analysis. ## A Custom Solution I built a **P&C Severity CPI** that reweights Bureau of Labor Statistics CPI components to better approximate the inflation experienced in insurance claims payments. ### Index Weights | Category | Weight | P&C Rationale | |----------|--------|---------------| | Auto Repair | 20% | Parts, labor, glass, paint for auto PD and collision | | Physicians | 15% | Medical evaluations, IMEs, treatment costs in BI claims | | Hospital | 15% | Inpatient costs for severe bodily injury claims | | Shelter | 20% | Proxy for construction/rebuild costs (lumber, labor) | | Core CPI (Wages) | 15% | Legal fees, adjusters, administrative costs | | Core CPI (General) | 15% | Residual services, general inflation exposure | ### The Formula The index is calculated as: $$\text{P\&C\_CPI}(t) = \sum_i w_i \times \frac{\text{CPI}_i(t)}{\text{CPI}_i(2010)} \times 100$$ Where: - $w_i$ = weight assigned to component $i$ (summing to 1.0) - $\text{CPI}_i(t)$ = BLS CPI index value for component $i$ at time $t$ - January 2010 is the base period (= 100) ## Current Results ::: {#2c281e17 .cell execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ## P&C Severity CPI vs CPI-U ::: {#e78a2196 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import matplotlib.dates as mdates # Load both datasets df_severity = pd.read_csv('data/severity_cpi.csv', parse_dates=['date']) df_cpi = pd.read_csv('data/All_Items.csv', parse_dates=['date']) # Rebase CPI-U to Jan 2010 = 100 base_value = df_cpi[df_cpi['date'] == '2010-01-01']['value'].values[0] df_cpi['CPI_U_rebased'] = (df_cpi['value'] / base_value) * 100 # Merge datasets df = df_severity.merge(df_cpi[['date', 'CPI_U_rebased']], on='date', how='inner') fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5)) # Plot CPI-U (solid blue line) ax.plot(df['date'], df['CPI_U_rebased'], color='#1e3a8a', linewidth=2.5, linestyle='-', label='CPI-U (All Items)') # Plot P&C Severity CPI (dotted red line) ax.plot(df['date'], df['P&C_Severity_CPI'], color='#dc2626', linewidth=2.5, linestyle=':', label='P&C Severity CPI') # Formatting ax.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=12) ax.set_ylabel('Index (Jan 2010 = 100)', fontsize=12) ax.set_title('P&C Severity CPI vs CPI-U (2010 = 100)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') ax.legend(loc='upper left') ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3) # Format x-axis ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(2)) ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y')) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![The red dotted line shows the P&C-weighted severity index. The blue line shows CPI-U (all items). When the gap widens, claims inflation is outpacing general inflation.](index_files/figure-html/cell-3-output-1.png){width=759 height=422} ::: ::: ## Annual Growth Rates ::: {#f2d5d746 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt df = pd.read_csv('data/severity_cpi.csv', parse_dates=['date']) # Calculate YoY change df['YoY_Change'] = df['P&C_Severity_CPI'].pct_change(periods=12) * 100 df_yoy = df.dropna(subset=['YoY_Change']) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5)) colors = ['#22c55e' if x < 3 else '#eab308' if x < 5 else '#dc2626' for x in df_yoy['YoY_Change']] ax.bar(df_yoy['date'], df_yoy['YoY_Change'], color=colors, width=25, alpha=0.8) ax.axhline(y=0, color='black', linewidth=0.5) ax.axhline(y=3, color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='3% threshold') ax.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=12) ax.set_ylabel('YoY Change (%)', fontsize=12) ax.set_title('Annual Inflation Rate: P&C Severity CPI', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y') plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Year-over-year percentage change in P&C Severity CPI](index_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=759 height=422} ::: ::: ## Key Insights The P&C Severity CPI has consistently outpaced general CPI-U inflation since 2010, with notable acceleration during: 1. **2021-2022**: Supply chain disruptions drove auto parts and repair costs sharply higher 2. **2023-2024**: Medical inflation and construction labor costs remained elevated 3. **Current**: The gap between P&C severity inflation and CPI-U remains significant ## What This Index Doesn't Capture Important caveats: - **Social inflation** and litigation trends - **Regulatory changes** or fee schedule modifications - **Claim frequency** shifts - **Regional variation** Always supplement with loss development analysis and market-specific data. ## Learn More The full methodology, data pipeline, and source code are available on GitHub: [P&C Severity CPI Repository](https://github.com/YoramGilboa/p-c-cpi-blog) The index updates monthly via GitHub Actions, pulling fresh data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics API.
